summary: Jeff Atwood argues that as of 2007, the tech industry has entered a second dot-com bubble, but one with more sustainable fundamentals than the first. He recounts the warning signs he ignored in 2000 and the painful aftermath for developers when that bubble burst. The key differences this time include widespread broadband adoption (50% vs 5%), viable advertising revenue models, and dramatically cheaper infrastructure through commodity hardware and open source software. While acknowledging the new bubble appears more focused on building real businesses than quick riches, he challenges readers to learn from the past and approach this era more wisely.
key_points: The 2000 dot-com bust led to three dark years for developers: offshoring, devalued IT careers, and diminished opportunities
Broadband penetration jumping from 5% to 50% fundamentally changed the viability of internet business models
Advertising networks like Google AdSense finally gave startups a realistic path to revenue
Open source and commodity hardware dropped startup infrastructure costs to near zero
The new bubble appears more focused on sustainable businesses than get-rich-quick schemes
money_quotes: You simply couldn't work in the field without having something miraculous or catastrophic happen to you. Or both at once.
Despite all the warning signs, it never occurred to me that I was working in a bubble. Until it popped.
The three years after the bubble burst were dark, dark times for software developers.
Seven years later, we're now clearly in the throes of another dot-com bubble.
The greasy odor of get-rich-quick isn't quite as overpowering as it was in 1999.
Bubbles are exciting times. Fortunes are made and lost; careers built and destroyed.
What will you do differently in this bubble?
key_insight: Those who lived through the first dot-com crash have a responsibility to recognize bubble dynamics and make wiser choices this time around, even as the fundamentals appear stronger.
